sandbox

Table of contents

  1. Vector fields
  2. Dynamical systems, flows, and integral curves
  3. A first glimpse at variational principles: lagrangian mechanics
  4. The cotangent bundle
  5. A first glimpse at symplectic topology: hamiltonian mechanics

Vector fields

Dynamical systems, flows, and integral curves

Manifolds often manifest themselves in physical theories as configuration spaces, encoding the locations and positions (i.e., the configurations) of the various objects under consideration. For example, once an origin and global coordinate system is fixed, the configuration space of a system consisting of a single point mass in $3$-space is $\bbr^3$. More generally, a system consisting of $k$ point masses in $3$-space has configuration space $\bbr^{3k}$, where each particle requires $3$-coordiantes to specify its location. Typographically, configuration spaces are often represented as $Q$, and local coordinates are often written as

\begin{equation}\notag q^1,\ldots,q^n. \end{equation}

Local coordinates on a configuration space $Q$ are often called generalized position coordinates.

Given a fixed configuration $q_0$ in a configuration space $Q$, a smooth path

\begin{equation}\label{pos-coord-eqn} \gamma:\bbr \to Q, \quad t\mapsto \gamma(t), \end{equation}

with $\gamma(0)=q_0$ is interpreted as a time evolution or trajectory of the system, beginning in the configuration $q_0$. However, the configuration space $Q$ often comes with some sort of rule or law, which says that only certain trajectories are allowable or realizable by the system. For example, if we consider a system consisting of a single point mass moving in $3$-space under the influence of an external force, then Newton’s laws of motion dictate which trajectories are allowable in $Q = \bbr^3$ and which are not.

The laws governing the time evolution of a system are often encoded as extra structures on the configuration space $Q$, or on manifolds closely related to $Q$. In fact, the tangent bundle $T(Q)$ often places a central role in dynamical systems theory, due to the following fundamental result which states that smooth paths in the base manifold “lift” to smooth paths in the tangent bundle:

Theorem on Path Lifting. Let $M$ be a manifold. If $\gamma:\bbr \to M$ is a smooth path in $M$, then there is a unique smooth path $\tl{\gamma}: \bbr\to T(M)$ such that the diagram \begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ & \color{white}T(M) \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\pi} \\ \color{white}\bbr \ar@[white][r]_{\color{white}\gamma} \ar@[white][ur]^{\color{white}\tl{\gamma}} & \color{white}M } \end{xy} \end{equation} commutes, where $\pi$ is the natural projection.

The theorem is proved by giving an explicit formula for the lift $\tl{\gamma}$, as

\begin{equation}\notag \tl{\gamma}(t) = (\gamma(t), \dot{\gamma}(t)), \end{equation}

where the “dot” denotes a time derivative:

\begin{equation}\notag \dot{\gamma}(t) \defeq \frac{\text{d}\gamma}{\text{d}t}(t). \end{equation}

One may show easily that $\tl{\gamma}$ is smooth, which completes the proof. Q.E.D.

If $Q$ is the configuration space of a dynamical system, then its tangent bundle $T(Q)$ is called the state space of the system, or sometimes the velocity phase space. As we learned in the post on tangent bundles, the generalized position coordinates \eqref{pos-coord-eqn} serve as “horizontal” local coordinates on $T(Q)$, while the remaining coordinates

\begin{equation}\label{vel-coord-eqn} v^1,\ldots,v^n \end{equation}

on $T(Q)$ may be taken as the “vertical” local coordinates. In the physical language, the coordinates \eqref{vel-coord-eqn} are often called generalized velocity coordinates, though they may not literally represent velocities.

The Theorem on Path Lifting shows that time evolutions in a configuration space $Q$, realized as smooth paths in $Q$, may equivalently be realized as time evolutions in the state space $T(Q)$. The advantage of considering the state space in place of the configuration space is that the former allows us to tie together relationships between (generalized) positions and (generalized) velocities in a very natural manner, as both appear as coordinates on the state space. Since the laws that govern the time evolution of a system often depend on both the (generalized) positions and (generalized) velocities of the system, these laws often appear as extra structures on the state space $T(Q)$, rather than $Q$.

For our first example, let’s show that Newton’s Second Law of Motion appears very naturally as a vector field on a state space.

Newton’s Second Law of Motion. Suppose that we have a point mass (i.e., a “particle”) moving in $3$-space under the influence of a force $F$. By fixing an origin and a global coordinate system, we may take the configuration space to be $Q=\bbr^3$. The trajectory of the particle through $Q$ is encoded as a smooth path

\begin{equation}\notag \bbr \to Q, \quad t \mapsto q(t), \end{equation}

which Newton tells us must be a solution to the second-order differential equation

\begin{equation}\notag m\ddot{q}(t) = F(q(t)), \end{equation}

where $m$ is the mass of the particle.

Turning to the state space $T(Q) = \bbr^6$, I claim that Newton’s law manifests itself as the vector field $X$ on $T(Q)$ defined as

\begin{equation}\notag X(q,v) = (v, F(q)/m), \end{equation}

where $q\in \bbr^3$ and $v\in \bbr^3$. Furthermore, I claim that the allowable trajectories in $T(Q)$ are the so-called “integral curves” of $X$. By definition, an integral curve of $X$ is a smooth path $\gamma:\bbr \to T(Q)$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag X(\gamma(t)) = \dot{\gamma}(t) \end{equation}

for all $t$.

For proof, suppose that we decompose a smooth path $\gamma: \bbr \to T(Q)$ in components as

\begin{equation}\notag t\mapsto \gamma(t) = (q(t),v(t)). \end{equation}

If $\gamma$ is an integral curve of $X$ according to the above definition, then the components $q(t)$ and $v(t)$ are solutions to the system of differential equations given by

\begin{equation}\notag \dot{q}(t) = v(t) \quad \text{and} \quad m\dot{v}(t) = F(q(t)). \end{equation}

But this means that $t\mapsto q(t)$ is an allowable trajectory in $Q=\bbr^3$, consistent with Newton’s law.

The example suggests that the differential equations governing newtonian mechanical systems may be described as vector fields on state spaces, and the allowable trajectories are then integral curves of the vector fields. This is an example of an “extra structure” on a state space which encodes the dynamical laws governing a system. But vector fields are not the only types of structures that encode such laws — indeed, as we will see later, other types of dynamical laws arise from smooth functions on state spaces and certain variational principles.

Very roughly, if we take all the allowable trajectories of a given dynamical system, realized as smooth paths, and combine them together into a single object, we obtain the following general type of structure. The definition of this structure applies to general manifolds, not just configuration spaces and state spaces of dynamical systems.

Definition. Let $M$ be a manifold. A one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms of $M$ is a collection of smooth maps

\begin{equation}\notag \theta^t:M \to M, \end{equation}

one for every $t\in \bbr$, such that:

  1. For every $x\in M$, we have $\theta^0(x) = x$.
  2. For all $s,t\in \bbr$, we have $\theta^s \circ \theta^t = \theta^{s+t}$.
  3. The induced map \begin{equation}\label{actionMap-eqn} \theta: \bbr \times M \to M, \quad (t,x) \mapsto \theta(t,x)\defeq \theta^t(x) \end{equation} is smooth.

The identity in part (2.) is called the group law and the induced map \eqref{actionMap-eqn} is called the global flow (or simply flow) associated to the one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms.

Though the manifold $M$ need not be a configuration or state space of a dynamical system, adopting a dynamics-inspired viewpoint is still helpful in understanding the definition of a one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms. From this viewpoint, a one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms $\{\theta^t\}$ on a manifold $M$ is the most general type of structure that encodes a dynamical system modelled by $M$. Said differently, the dynamics of a system are often presented as a law that must be “solved” in order to find the allowable trajectories — then, in a sense, one-parameter groups of diffeomorphisms are the “ultimate solutions” to these dynamical laws.

So, given a manifold $M$ with one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms $\{\theta^t\}$, let’s agree to think of the points of $M$ as the “states” of some imaginary dynamical system. Then, given an initial state $x_0\in M$ at time $t=t_0$, for each $t\in \bbr$ the value of $\theta^t(x_0)$ is supposed to represent the state of the system at time $t+t_0$. The identity (1.) is then simply the statement that advancing the system ahead in time by a value $t=0$ doesn’t change the state of the system. In this context, the diffeomorphism $\theta^t$ is often called a $t$-advance operator or an evolution operator.

The group law (2.) is used to show that the system is deterministic, which means the entire evolution of the system is fully determined by the initial state $x_0$ at time $t=t_0$. To see how this works, we advance time to an intermediate value $t=t_1\geq t_0$ and obtain an intermediate state

\begin{equation}\notag x_1 \defeq \theta^{t_1-t_0}(x_0). \end{equation}

After this, suppose we advance time to a final value $t=t_2\geq t_1$, and obtain a final state

\begin{equation}\notag x_2 \defeq \theta^{t_2-t_1}(x_1). \end{equation}

We then have

\begin{equation}\notag x_2 = \theta^{t_2-t_1}(x_1) = \theta^{t_2-t_1}(\theta^{t_1-t_0}(x_0)) = \theta^{t_2-t_0}(x_0), \end{equation}

which shows that the final state $x_2$ does not depend on the intermediate state $x_1$, and is obtained directly from the initial state by advancing time from $t_0$ to $t_2$. The group law is sometimes called the Chapman-Kolmogorov law (see [?]), which is a name that also pops up in the theory of Markov chains (see [?]) where it is used to similar effect.

For each $x\in M$, there is an associated smooth path

\begin{equation}\label{trajectory-eqn} t\mapsto \theta^t(x) \end{equation}

in $M$. If for each such $x$ we define

\begin{equation}\notag X(x) = \frac{d}{dt} \theta^t(x)\big|_{t=0}, \end{equation}

then we obtain a smooth vector field $X$ on $M$ called the infinitesimal generator or the local law of evolution of the global flow. The formal relation between the trajectory \eqref{trajectory-eqn} and the infinitesimal generator is given in the next general definition and theorem. The definition will be familiar from our discussion on Newton’s Second Law of Motion.

Definition. Let $X$ be a smooth vector field on a manifold $M$. A smooth path $\gamma:\bbr \to M$ is called an integral curve of $X$ if \begin{equation}\notag X(\gamma(t)) = \dot{\gamma}(t) \end{equation} for all $t\in \bbr$.

Then, we have:

Theorem on Integral Curves and Infinitesimal Generators. Let $\theta$ be a global flow on $M$ with associated one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms $\{\theta^t\}$. Then, for fixed $x\in M$, the curve $t\mapsto \theta^t(x)$ is an integral curve of the infinitesimal generator $X$.

The proof is just a simple application of the group law and the chain rule. Indeed, for each $t\in \bbr$, we have:

\begin{equation}\notag X(\theta^t(x)) = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}s} \theta^s(\theta^t(x)) \big|_{s=0} = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}s} \theta^{s+t}(x) \big|_{s=0} = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\theta^t(x). \end{equation}

The desired claim then follows, Q.E.D.

Thus, every global flow on $M$ yields a vector field by passing to the associated infinitesimal generator. Conversely, as we will see below, under certain hypotheses it is possible to reverse this correspondence by associating a global flow with every vector field….

A first glimpse at variational principles: lagrangian mechanics

Our work in the previous section shows that newtonian mechanical systems may be realized as certain vector fields on state space manifolds, and that the evolutions of the systems are described by integral curves of the vector fields. In this section, I want to describe a different (but equivalent) formulation of mechanics called lagrangian mechanics.

The lagrangian formulation has a very different feel to it, compared to the newtonian one. In contrast to the “geometric” flavor of newtonian mechanics, dominated as it is by vector fields and the like, lagrangian mechanics is based fundamentally on a certain variational principle, giving it a distinctly “analytic” feel. Indeed, classical newtonian mechanics is sometimes called vector mechanics, while lagrangian mechanics is sometimes called analytic mechanics. That these two formulations of mechanics are actually equivalent is (I think) quite amazing.

Later, after our brief excursion into lagrangian mechanics, we will come full circle back to vector fields and flows, when we describe the transition from lagrangian mechanics to the (equivalent) formulation of hamiltonian mechanics. The latter is a very (very!) geometric theory — in particular, it is often cited as the primary origin of the mathematical fields of symplectic geometry and topology.

The lagrangian story begins with the following definition, which is so general that it’s hard to imagine it actually has any real content:

Definition. A langrangian system is a pair $(Q,L)$, where $Q$ is a smooth manifold and $L$ is a smooth function $L:T(Q) \to \bbr$. As in the previous section, the manifold $Q$ is called the configuration space of the system and the tangent bundle $T(Q)$ is called the state space of the system (or the velocity phase space). The function $L$ is called the lagrangian of the system.

The points of the state space of a lagrangian system are, as the name suggests, supposed to represent the states of the system. The time evolution of a state is then realized as a smooth path in the state space, beginning at the given state. In contrast to newtonian systems, however, these paths are not (at least at first glance) realized as integral curves of a vector field. Rather, they are described and identified according to the following fundamental variational principle:

Hamilton’s Principle. Let $(Q,L)$ be a lagrangian system. The allowable smooth paths $\gamma:[t_0,t_1]\to Q$ describing the time evolution of the system are exactly those paths that are stationary points of the action integral $A_L$, defined as \begin{equation}\notag A_L(\gamma) = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} L(\gamma(t),\dot{\gamma}(t)) \ \text{d}t. \end{equation}

To say that a smooth path $\gamma$ is a stationary point of $A_L$ means that, if we infinitesimally deform $\gamma$ to obtain a second path $\gamma’$, then the two integrals $A_L(\gamma)$ and $A_L(\gamma’)$ do not change. There’s an analogy here with basic one-variable functions: The point $x_0$ is a stationary point of the function $y=f(x)$ precisely when $f’(x_0)=0$, which expresses the fact that (first-order) infinitesimal perturbations away from $x_0$ do not change the value of $f(x_0)$. In our situation, there is a type of derivative of $A_L$ called the first variation, and the path $\gamma$ is a stationary point of $A_L$ if and only if the first variation of $A_L$ vanishes when evaluated at $\gamma$.

I will leave it to you to learn about the formalism of first variations on your own (find a book on the calculus of variations). The only thing that we will need is the fact that stationary points of $A_L$ are solutions to a certain famous system of differential equations:

Theorem on Euler-Lagrange Equations. Let $(Q,L)$ be an $n$-dimensional lagrangian system. A smooth path $\gamma$ in $Q$ is an allowable trajectory of the system according to Hamilton’s Principle if and only if it is a solution to the Euler-Lagrange equations: \begin{equation}\notag \frac{\bd L}{\bd q^i}(\gamma(t),\dot{\gamma}(t)) = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(\frac{\bd L}{\bd v^i}(\gamma(t),\dot{\gamma}(t)) \right), \quad i=1,\ldots,n. \end{equation}

These equations are local expressions for the Euler-Lagrange equations, as you can see from the presence of the generalized position coordinates and the generalized velocity coordinates discussed in the previous section. The global Euler-Lagrange equations are obtained from these local expressions by “gluing together on overlaps,” in a suitable sense. We won’t worry too much about this, however, so don’t think too hard about it (if you don’t want to).

The lagrangian $L$ of a lagrangian system is the conceptual analog of the force $F$ of a newtonian system; both of them are the “origin” or the “driver” of the dynamics. While the force $F$ governs the dynamics through Newton’s Second Law, the lagrangian $L$ governs the dynamics through the Euler-Lagrange equations. The link between the two formulations of mechanics is made when we assume that $F$ is conservative, with potential $V$ (i.e., $F = -\nabla V$), and we define a lagrangian $L$ by

\begin{equation}\label{lagrangian-eqn} L = K - V, \end{equation}

where $K$ is the kinetic energy of the system. For simplicity, let’s suppose that our system consists of a single particle of mass $m$ moving in the configuration space $Q=\bbr^3$, subject to the force $F$. Then, we have

\begin{equation}\notag L(q,v) = \frac{1}{2}m||v||^2 - V(q). \end{equation}

If

\begin{equation}\notag \gamma: \bbr \to Q, \quad t\mapsto \gamma(t) = (\gamma^1(t),\gamma^2(t),\gamma^3(t)) \end{equation}

is the trajectory of the particle, then the Euler-Lagrange equations take the form

\begin{equation}\notag -\frac{\bd V}{\bd q^i}(\gamma(t)) = m\ddot{\gamma}^i(t) \quad (i=1,2,3). \end{equation}

But these are exactly (the components of) Newton’s Second Law. While this argument shows that newtonian mechanics may be reformulated in the lagrangian framework only for this very special dynamical system consisting of a single particle, the equivalence of the two formulations is true much more generally.

Let’s consider an example where the configuration space $Q$ is non-euclidean. Suppose that we have a particle $p$ constrained to move along the sphere $S^2\subset \bbr^3$, acted on by gravity and the constraining force:

We have coordinatized the system so that the equatorial plane of $S^2$ is the $xy$-plane, while the positive $z$-axis points downward, in the direction of gravity. We let $\theta$ and $\varphi$ denote the spherical coordinates of the particle $p$, shown as the white angles in the figure. The angle $\theta$ is the azimuthal angle measured from the positive $x$-axis, while $\varphi$ is the polar angle measured from the positive $z$-axis. While $p$ moves in the ambient euclidean space $\bbr^3$, the only physically realizable positions lie on the sphere $S^2$, and hence the configuration space of the system is $Q = S^2$. For mathematical simplicity, we will suppose that all our constants are “geometric,” so that the particle has unit mass and the acceleration due to gravity is $g=1$.

The state space is the tangent bundle $T(S^2)$, which is a submanifold of the “ambient” state space $T(\bbr^3) = \bbr^6$. The kinetic energy of $p$ computed in the global euclidean coordinates of $T(\bbr^3)$ is given by the familiar formula

\begin{equation}\notag K(x^1,x^2,x^3,v^1,v^2,v^3) = \frac{1}{2}\big((v^1)^2 + (v^2)^2 + (v^3)^2\big), \end{equation}

while the gravitational potential energy is given by

\begin{equation}\notag V(x^1,x^2,x^3,v^1,v^2,v^3) = -z + 1, \end{equation}

where I’ve taken $V=0$ at the equilibrium position $(0,0,1)$. We may restrict $K$ and $V$ to obtain smooth functions

\begin{equation}\notag K,V: T(S^2) \to \bbr, \end{equation}

and then the lagrangian $L:T(S^2) \to \bbr$ is defined above as the difference $L= K-V$. Notice that I have effectively ignored the constraining force that keeps the particle on the sphere $S^2$; this constraining force is taken into account when we declare the configuration space to be $Q=S^2$.

Now, in order to obtain the allowable trajectories of the system, we must express the Euler-Lagrange equations in local coordinates on the state space $T(S^2)$. To do so, we first recall that the map

\begin{equation}\notag (\theta,\varphi) \mapsto (\cos{\theta}\sin{\varphi},\sin{\theta}\sin{\varphi},\cos{\varphi}) \end{equation}

provides a local parametrization of $S^2$, suitably restricted to an open subset of ordered pairs $(\theta,\varphi)\in \bbr^2$. It follows from our previous post on tangent bundles, that the global pushforward of this map provides a local parametrization of the tangent bundle $T(S^2)$; if we write $\theta,\varphi$ and $\dot{\theta},\dot{\varphi}$ as the corresponding local coordinates on the tangent bundle, then I will leave it to you to check that we have

\begin{equation}\notag K(\theta,\varphi,\dot{\theta},\dot{\varphi}) = \frac{1}{2}(\dot{\varphi}^2 +\dot{\theta}^2\sin^2{(\varphi)}) \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag V(\theta,\varphi,\dot{\theta},\dot{\varphi}) = -\cos{\varphi}+1 \end{equation}

in these coordinates. Here, $\dot{\theta}$ and $\dot{\varphi}$ do not represent time derivatives; rather, they are purely formal symbols that make it clear that $\dot{\theta}$ is the (generalized) velocity coordinate corresponding to the (generalized) position coordinate $\theta$, and similarly for $\dot{\varphi}$ and $\varphi$.

If the local coordinates are functions of time, representing an allowable trajectory of the particle in state space $T(S^2)$, then they must be solutions to the Euler-Lagrange equations

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{\bd L}{\bd \theta} = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(\frac{\bd L}{\bd \dot{\theta}} \right) \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\bd L}{\bd \varphi} = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(\frac{\bd L}{\bd \dot{\varphi}} \right). \end{equation}

Carrying through with the differentiations, the first equation yields

\begin{equation}\notag 0 = \ddot{\theta}\sin^2{\varphi} + 2\dot{\theta}\dot{\varphi}\sin{\varphi}\cos{\varphi} \end{equation}

while the second equation yields

\begin{equation}\notag \dot{\theta}^2 \sin{\varphi}\cos{\varphi} - \sin{\varphi} = \ddot{\varphi}. \end{equation}

The cotangent bundle

It will be convenient to begin in the “local case,” where we assume that our configuration space is $Q = \bbr^n$, and our state space is the tangent bundle $T(Q) = \bbr^{2n}$. We suppose that the global coordinates of $T(Q)$ are given by

\begin{equation}\notag q^1,\ldots,q^n,v^1,\ldots,v^n, \end{equation}

where, in the geometric language, $q^1,\ldots,q^n$ are the horizontal coordinates and $v^1,\ldots,v^n$ are the vertical coordinates. In the physical language, $q^1,\ldots,q^n$ are the generalized position coordinates and $v^1,\ldots,v^n$ are the generalized velocity coordinates.

Let $Q$ be an $n$-dimensional manifold embedded in $\bbr^r$, and consider the natural projection $\pi: T(Q) \to Q$. Clearly the pushforward $\pi_{\ast,(q,v)}$ is surjective at every point $(q,v) \in T(Q)$, and hence $\pi$ is a submersion. For each $q\in Q$, it then follows that the fiber

\begin{equation}\notag \pi^{-1}(q) = \{q\}\times T_q(Q) \end{equation}

is an $n$-dimensional (closed) submanifold of the tangent bundle $T(Q)$. In fact, the fiber $\pi^{-1}(q)$, as a smooth submanifold of $T(Q)$, has global coordinates given by the vertical coordinates $v^1,\ldots,v^n$ over an open parametrizable subset of $q\in Q$. In particular, there is an isomorphism

\begin{equation}\notag T_q(Q) \xrightarrow{\cong} T_{(q,v)}(\pi^{-1}(q)) \end{equation}

of vector spaces that does…

\begin{equation} T_q(Q) \xrightarrow{\cong} T_{(q,v)} \left( \pi^{-1}(q)\right), \quad w\mapsto (0,w) \end{equation}

of vector spaces.

Now, let $L:T(Q) \to \bbr$ be a smooth function. Since the fiber $\pi^{-1}(q)$ is a smooth submanifold of $T(Q)$, the restriction

\begin{equation}\notag L|_{\pi^{-1}(q)}: \pi^{-1}(q) \xrightarrow{\text{inclusion}} T(Q) \xrightarrow{L} \bbr \end{equation}

is smooth. If we pass to the differential at a point $(q,v)$ in the fiber, we get a linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \text{d}\left( L|_{\pi^{-1}(q)} \right)_{(q,v)}: T_{(q,v)}(\pi^{-1}(q)) \to \bbr. \end{equation}

However, as was saw above, we may use the global coordinates on $T_q(Q)$ to identify the double tangent space $T_v(T_q(Q))$ with $T_q(Q)$, and thus this last map may be identified with a covector

\begin{equation}\notag \text{d}\left( L|_{T_q(Q)} \right)_v: T_q(Q) \to \bbr \end{equation}

in the cotangent space $T^q(Q)$.

Definition. Let the notation be as above. The smooth map \begin{equation}\notag FL: T(Q) \to T^\ast(Q), \quad (q,v) \mapsto \left(q, \text{d}\left( L|_{T_q(Q)} \right)_v\right) \end{equation} is called the fiber derivative or Legendre transform of $L$.

It will be helpful to find a local expression for the fiber derivative $FL$. To do so, let $q^1,\ldots,q^n,v^1,\ldots,v^n$ be the horizontal/vertical coordinates on $T(Q)$ above an open parametrizable subset of $q$, and let $q^1,\ldots,q^n,p_1,\ldots,p_n$ be the horizontal/vertical coordinates on $T^\ast(Q)$. Recall that

\begin{equation}\notag p^i = \text{d}q_i \end{equation}

We have that

\begin{equation}\notag \text{d}\left( L|_{T_q(Q)} \right) = \frac{\bd L}{\bd v^i} \text{d}v^i \end{equation}

A first glimpse at symplectic topology: hamiltonian mechanics

Appendix: calculus of variations

Definition. Let $[a,b]$ be a closed interval on the real line. For each $n\geq 0$, we define $C^n[a,b]$ to be the set of all functions $y:[a,b]\to \bbr$ for which all derivatives

\begin{equation}\notag y,y’,y’’,\ldots,y^{(n)} \end{equation}

exist and are continuous on $[a,b]$. (In the case that $n=0$, this means only that $y$ is continuous and places no requirements on the existence or continuity of the derivatives of $y$.) Given $y\in C^n[a,b]$, we define

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{y}_n = \sum_{k=0}^n \max_{a\leq x \leq b}|y^{(k)}(x)|, \end{equation}

where, as usual, we set $y^{(0)} = y$. Notice that the maximums all exist, since we are dealing with continuous real-valued functions on a compact set.

The set $C^n[a,b]$ carries four important structures, three algebraic and one geometric:

  1. It carries an additive structure, realized via the pointwise sum operation $(y_1,y_2) \mapsto y_1+y_2$ defined by \begin{equation}\notag (y_1+y_1)(x) = y_1(x) + y_2(x), \quad \forall x\in [a,b]. \end{equation}
  2. It carries a multiplicative structure, realized via the pointwise multiplication operation $(y_1,y_2)\mapsto y_1y_2$ defined by \begin{equation}\notag (y_1y_2)(x) = y_1(x)y_2(x), \quad \forall x\in [a,b]. \end{equation}
  3. It carries an action of the additive group $\bbr$, realized via the operation $(c,y) \mapsto cy$ defined by \begin{equation}\notag (cy)(x) = cy(x), \quad \forall x\in [a,b]. \end{equation}
  4. It carries a geometric structure, realized via the metric \begin{equation}\label{metric-eqn} (y_1,y_2) \mapsto \norm{y_1-y_2}_n. \end{equation}

Notice that the geometric structure is intertwined with the algebraic structures, in the sense that the formula \eqref{metric-eqn} for the metric directly uses the subtraction operation. In fact, each of these four structures is tightly linked with all the others, in the sense that $C^n[a,b]$ has the extremely rich structure of a (real, unital, commutative) Banach algebra. This means:

  1. The additive and $\bbr$-action structures together make $C^n[a,b]$ a real vector space. The function $||\cdot||_n$ then makes $C^n[a,b]$ a normed vector space.
  2. The multiplicative and vector space structures together make $C^n[a,b]$ a (real, commutative, unital) algebra.
  3. With respect to the metric defined by \eqref{metric-eqn}, $C^n[a,b]$ is complete, i.e., all Cauchy sequences converge.
  4. The norm $||\cdot ||_n$ interacts coherently with the multiplicative structure, in the form of submultiplicativity: \begin{equation}\notag \norm{y_1y_2}_n \leq \norm{y_1}_n \norm{y_2}_n, \quad \forall y_1,y_2\in C^n[a,b]. \end{equation} This property implies, among other things, that the multiplication operation is continuous.

In our work, we will primarily be interested in $C^0[a,b]$ and $C^1[a,b]$ consisting, respectively, of all continuous and continuously differentiable real-valued functions on $[a,b]$.

We now define the main objects of study in the calculus of variations:

Definition. Let $[a,b]$ be a closed interval on the real line $\bbr$. A functional on $C^n[a,b]$ is a function $J: D \to \bbr$, where $D$ is a nonempty subset of $C^n[a,b]$.

Be careful to to note even though a functional $J$ as in the definition is called a functional on $C^n[a,b]$, its domain $D$ may be a proper subset of $C^n[a,b]$.

We shall be concerned mostly with functionals fitting the following template: Let $D$ be a nonempty subset of $C^1[a,b]$ and $F:\bbr^3 \to \bbr$ a continuous function. We then define a functional

\begin{equation}\label{template-eqn} J: D\to \bbr, \quad J(y) = \int_a^b F(x,y,y’) \ \d x. \end{equation}

Here, for notational brevity, we are writing the integrand as $F(x,y,y’)$, which stands for $F(x,y(x),y’(x))$.

The first thing that we want to prove about these special types of functionals is that they are continuous:

Theorem. Provided that the domain $D$ is a bounded subset of $C^1[a,b]$, the functional $J$ defined by \eqref{template-eqn} is uniformly continuous.

Since $D$ is bounded with respect to $||\cdot ||_1$, for each $y\in D$ the mapping

\begin{equation}\notag x \mapsto (x,y(x),y’(x)) \end{equation}

sends the interval $[a,b]$ into a bounded subset of $\bbr^3$. We may thus replace the domain of $F$ with a compact subset of $\bbr^3$ and thereby assume that $F$ is uniformly continuous. Therefore, given $\dev>0$, there is a $\delta>0$ such that

\begin{equation}\label{implication-eqn} (x_1-x_2)^2 + (u_1-u_2)^2 +(v_1-v_2)^2 < 2\delta^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad |F(x_1,u_1,v_1) - F(x_2,u_2,v_2)| < \frac{\dev}{b-a} \end{equation}

for all $(x_1,u_1,v_1)$ and $(x_2,u_2,v_2)$ in the (compact) domain of $F$. In particular, if we have

\begin{equation}\label{ineq-eqn} \norm{y_1-y_2}_1 < \delta \end{equation}

for $y_1,y_2 \in D$, then

\begin{equation}\notag (y_1(x)-y_2(x))^2 + (y’_1(x)-y_2’(x))^2 < 2\delta^2 \end{equation}

for all $x\in [a,b]$. In view of \eqref{implication-eqn}, this implies that

\begin{equation}\notag |F(x,y_1(x),y’_1(x)) - F(x,y_2(x),y_2’(x))| < \dev/(b-a) \end{equation}

for all $x\in [a,b]$. Thus, for those $y_1,y_2$ such that \eqref{ineq-eqn} holds, we have

\begin{align} |J(y_1)-J(y_2)| &= \left| \int_a^b (F(x,y_1(x),y’_1(x)) - F(x,y_2(x),y_2’(x))) \ \d x \right| \notag \\ &\leq \int_a^b |F(x,y_1(x),y_1’(x)) - F(x,y_2(x),y_2’(x))| \ \d x \notag \\ &< \dev, \notag \end{align}

which shows $J$ is uniformly continuous. Q.E.D.

Having defined functionals, we now define their variations:

Definition. Let $J:D \to \bbr$ be a functional on $C^n[a,b]$. A set of increments for $J$ is a subset $I\subseteq C^n[a,b]$ such that:

  1. The constant function $0\in C^n[a,b]$ is a limit point of $I$.
  2. For each $y\in D$ and $h\in I$, we have $y+h\in D$.

We shall say $J$ is differentiable at $y\in D$ with increments $I$ if $I$ is a set of increments for $J$ and there exists a linear functional

\begin{equation}\notag \delta J_y : I\to \bbr \end{equation}

such that

\begin{equation}\notag J(y+h) - J(y) = \delta J_y(h) + o(h), \end{equation}

where $h\mapsto o(h)$ is a functional defined for $h\in I$ such that $o(h)/\norm{h}_n\to 0$ as $h\to 0$.

The linear functional $\delta J_y$, provided that it exists, is called a (first) variation of $J$ at $y$.

First, to be clear, to say that $\delta J_y$ is linear means that:

  1. $\delta J_y(h_1+h_2) = \delta J_y(h_1) + \delta J_y(h_2)$ for all $h_1,h_2\in I$, provided that $h_1+h_2 \in I$.
  2. $\delta J_y(ch) = c \delta J_y(h)$ for all $c\in \bbr$ and $h\in I$, provided that $ch\in I$.

Also, when I write “$h\to 0$” as in the definition, I mean that we are letting $h\to 0$ over $h\in I$.

Now, a few remarks are in order, to help explain this definition of variation. Derivatives and differentials of all sorts are all local objects, defined pointwise on the domains of functions. The domains are often assumed to be open, so that each point has a sufficient number of nearby points to assure that limits are well-defined, and so that increments may be taken in any direction whatsoever. However, even in the simplest of cases, the domains of our functionals will not be open, and thus these more familiar and well-established definitions of derivatives and differentials will not work.

A simple example will illustrate my point. Let’s suppose that we are given two fixed points $y_1,y_2\in \bbr$, and that we define the set

\begin{equation}\notag D = \left\{ y\in C^1[a,b] \mid y(a) = y_1, \ y(b) = y_2\right\}, \end{equation}

elements of which may be visualized as continuously differentiable curves $x\mapsto (x,y(x))$ in the plane with end points $(a,y_1)$ and $(b,y_2)$. We may then define the functional

\begin{equation}\label{arc-eqn} J:D \to \bbr, \quad J(y) = \int_a^b \sqrt{1+{y’}^2} \ \d x, \end{equation}

where, following the standard practice, we’ve written the integrand with the argument of $y’$ (namely, $x$) omitted. Note that this functional fits the special template mentioned above in \eqref{template-eqn}. In fact, the value $J(y)$ is nothing but the arc length of the associated curve $x\mapsto (x,y(x))$, and finding minimum values of $J$ is one of the first examples given in any presentation of the calculus of variations.

Naturally, if $y^\ast$ is a minimum value of $J$ (or any other type of stationary point), then the difference

\begin{equation}\label{diff1-eqn} J(y^\ast + h) - J(y^\ast) \end{equation}

should be very close to $0$, provided that the increment $h$ is small. The goal is then to cook up some sort of derivative or differential that makes this precise, i.e., some sort of object which captures the first-order part of the difference \eqref{diff1-eqn}. But this is exactly what the variation $\delta J_{y^\ast}$ is designed to do!

However, take care to notice two things: First, the domain $D$ is not an open subset of $C^1[a,b]$, and so our definition of variation of a general functional must not require that the functional be defined on an open set. And second—what exactly do we mean by the “increment” $h$ in \eqref{diff1-eqn}? Clearly, it does not make sense to allow $h$ to be an arbitrary function in $C^1[a,b]$, for then $y^\ast + h$ might not be a curve with its end points at $(a,y_1)$ and $(b,y_2)$, and we don’t care about the value $J(y^\ast + h)$ on these inadmissible curves. Thus, in addition to carefully specifiying the domains of the functionals in our definition of variation of a general functional, we must also be very careful to specify the admissible increments. For our arc-length functional $J$ under consideration, the right choice for increments is

\begin{equation}\notag I = \left\{ h\in C^1[a,b] \mid h(a) = h(b) = 0\right\}. \end{equation}

In the general case, the requirement that the constant function $0$ be a limit point of a set of increments simply assures that there are “enough” increments for “$h\to 0$” to make sense with $h$ restricted to lie in $I$, and the requirement that $y+h\in D$ for all $h\in I$ is self-explanatory, since we need the difference $J(y+h) - J(y)$ to make sense.

Thus, our definition of the variation of a functional really is quite natural!

In essentially all of the cases that we will consider, the sets of increments $I$ won’t just have the constant function $0$ as a limit point, but $I$ will actually be star-shaped at $0$. This means that for each $h\in I$ and for all $\dev\in [0,1]$, the function $\dev h$ lies in $I$. In other words, given $h\in I$, the straight-line path from $h$ to $0$ lies in $I$. In these cases, a variation may be computed as a sort of directional derivative:

Theorem. Let $J:D \to \bbr$ be a functional on $C^n[a,b]$ and suppose that $J$ is differentiable at a point $y\in D$ with increments $I$. Provided that $I$ is star-shaped at $0$, there is an equality

\begin{equation}\notag \delta J_y(h) = \frac{\d}{\d \dev} \Big|_{\dev=0} J(y+\dev h) \end{equation}

for each $h\in I$. (Existence of the derivative on the right-hand side is part of the conclusion.)

By hypothesis, we have

\begin{equation}\notag J(y+h) - J(y) = \delta J_y(h) + o(h), \end{equation}

where $o(h)/\norm{h}_n \to 0$ as $h\to 0$. By replacing $h$ with $\dev h$ and dividing by $\dev$ ($\neq 0$), we get

\begin{equation}\label{tree-eqn} \frac{J(y+\dev h) - J(y)}{\dev} = \delta J_y(h) + \frac{o(\dev h)}{\dev}. \end{equation}

Then, sending $\dev\to 0$ also sends $\dev h\to 0$, and hence

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{o(\dev h)}{|\dev| \norm{h}_n} = \frac{ o(\dev h)}{\norm{\dev h}_n} \to 0. \end{equation}

But this means $o(\dev h)/\dev \to 0$ too, so that from \eqref{tree-eqn} we get

\begin{equation}\notag \delta J_y(h) = \lim_{\dev \to 0} \frac{J(y+\dev h) - J(y)}{\dev} = \frac{\d}{\d \dev} \Big|_{\dev=0} J(y+\dev h), \end{equation}

as desired. Q.E.D.

Before some important examples of variations, let’s address uniqueness:

Exercise. Let $J:D \to \bbr$ be a functional on $C^n[a,b]$, $y\in D$, and $I$ a set of increments for $J$. If both $A_y:I \to \bbr$ and $B_y:I \to \bbr$ are variations of $J$ at $y$, prove that $A_y = B_y$.

For our first example of an explict computation of a variation, we consider a general fixed-endpoint type functional, of which the arc-length functional \eqref{arc-eqn} mentioned above is a specific example. This is the setting of the “simplest variational problem,” according to Gelfand and Fomin’s book.

Theorem/Definition (The Simplest Variational Problem: fixed-endpoint functionals). For fixed $y_1,y_2 \in \bbr$, define

\begin{equation}\notag D = \left\{ y\in C^1[a,b] \mid y(a) = y_1, \ y(b) = y_2\right\}. \end{equation}

Suppose that $F:\bbr^3 \to \bbr$ is a smooth function and define the functional

\begin{equation}\label{example-eqn} J: D \to \bbr, \quad J(y) = \int_a^b F(x,y,y’) \ \d x. \end{equation}

Such a functional is called a fixed-endpoint functional. Then:

  1. The functional $J$ is differentiable at all $y\in D$ with increments \begin{equation}\notag I = \left\{ h\in C^1[a,b] \mid h(a) = h(b) = 0\right\}, \end{equation} and furthermore \begin{equation}\label{form-eqn} \delta J_y(h) = \int_a^b \left(\frac{\bd F}{\bd y}(x,y,y’)h + \frac{\bd F}{\bd y’}(x,y,y’)h’ \right) \ \d x. \end{equation}
  2. The variation vanishes $\delta J_y=0$ if and only if $y$ is a solution of the Euler-Lagrange equation \begin{equation}\label{el-eqn} \frac{\bd F}{\bd y}(x,y,y’) - \frac{\d}{\d x} \frac{\bd F}{\bd y’}(x,y,y’) =0. \end{equation}

Before starting the proof, take note that the partial derivatives

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{\bd F}{\bd y}(x,y,y’) \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\bd F}{\bd y’}(x,y,y’) \end{equation}

in \eqref{form-eqn} and \eqref{el-eqn} are horrible abuses of notation, which are unfortunately quite common in the calculus of variations. What is really meant is

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{\bd F}{\bd u}(x,y(x),y’(x)) \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\bd F}{\bd v}(x,y(x),y’(x)), \end{equation}

where $F$ is viewed as a function of the independent variables $x,u,v$, i.e., $F(x,u,v)$. Also, as you will observe in the proof below, full smoothness of $F$ isn’t needed. We really only need that all second-order partial derivatives of $F$ exist and are continuous.

Now, beginning the proof of (1.), note that for fixed $y\in D$ and any $h\in I$, by Taylor’s Theorem we may write

\begin{align} F(x,y+h,y’+h’) - F(x,y,y’) &= \frac{\bd F}{\bd y}(x,y,y’)h + \frac{\bd F}{\bd y’}(x,y,y’)h’ \label{cube-eqn} \\ &\quad+ \alpha(x,y+h,y’+h’)h^2 + \beta(x,y+h,y’+h’)hh’ \notag \\ &\quad + \gamma(x,y+h,y’+h’){h’}^2 \notag \end{align}

for some smooth functions $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$. In these expressions, all functions $y,y’,h,h’$ are understood to be evaluated at the point $x\in [a,b]$. Now, I claim that

\begin{equation}\label{window1-eqn} \frac{1}{\norm{h}_1}\int_a^b \alpha(x,y+h,y’+h’)h^2 \ \d x \to 0 \end{equation}

as $h\to 0$. To prove this, note that it suffices to prove

\begin{equation}\label{window-eqn} \norm{h}_1 \int_a^b |\alpha(x,y+h,y’+h’)| \ \d x \to 0 \end{equation}

as $h\to 0$. But the integral may be considered a functional defined on a (bounded) subset of $I$, and is thus continuous with respect to $h$. Therefore,

\begin{equation}\notag \lim_{h\to 0} \int_a^b |\alpha(x,y+h,y’+h’)| \ \d x = \int_a^b |\alpha(x,y,y’)| \ \d x, \end{equation}

from which the convergence \eqref{window-eqn}—and therefore also the convergence \eqref{window1-eqn}—follows. A similar argument demonstrates the convergence \eqref{window1-eqn} where $\alpha$ is replaced with $\beta$ or $\gamma$, and $h^2$ with $hh’$ or ${h’}^2$. It then follows immediately from \eqref{cube-eqn} that $J$ is differentiable at $y$ with increments $I$, and that

\begin{equation}\notag \delta J_y(h) = \int_a^b \left(\frac{\bd F}{\bd y}(x,y,y’)h + \frac{\bd F}{\bd y’}(x,y,y’)h’ \right) \ \d x, \end{equation}

which establishes (1.). The proof of (2.) is a simple argument using integration by parts and the so-called fundamental lemma of the calculus of variations. You may cook up your own proof, or look one up in Gelfand and Fomin’s book. Q.E.D.

We obtained the formula \eqref{form-eqn} in the course of proving that $J$ was differentiable at $y$. However, if we had already known that $J$ was differentiable, this same formula would be obtainable by computing $\delta J_y(h)$ as the directional derivative

\begin{equation}\notag \delta J_y(h) = \frac{\d}{\d \dev} \Big|_{\dev=0} J(y+\dev h) \end{equation}

and pulling the derivative under the integral sign.

Definition. Let

\begin{equation}\notag J:D \to \bbr, \quad J(y) = \int_a^b F(x,y,y’) \ \d x, \end{equation}

be a fixed-endpoint functional on $C^1[a,b]$, for some smooth function $F:\bbr^3 \to \bbr$. Then solutions $y\in D$ to the Euler-Lagrange equation

\begin{equation}\label{el2-eqn} \frac{\bd F}{\bd y}(x,y,y’) - \frac{\d}{\d x} \frac{\bd F}{\bd y’}(x,y,y’) =0, \end{equation}

equipped some set of suitable boundary conditions, are called extremals of $J$.

Since the Euler-Lagrange equation is a second-order differential equation, it requires in general two boundary conditions. In the case of the fixed-endpoint functionals considered in the previous theorem, these boundary conditions are provided by $y(a)=y_1$ and $y(b)=y_2$.

The significance of extremals may be seen by considering the fixed-endpoint functionals, where, according to the previous theorem, such an extremal $y$ is a function at which the variation vanishes $\delta J_y=0$. Analogous to the familiar situation in single-variable calculus with derivatives in place of variations, the vanishing condition $\delta J_y=0$ is easily seen to be a necessary condition for $y$ to be either a (local) minimizer or maximizer of $J$. The extreme values are called weak extrema in the literature. In general, however, the vanishing condition $\delta J_y=0$ merely states that $J$ is stationary (to the first order) at $y$, and that $J$ might have a minimum at $y$, a maximum, or neither.

Exercise. Use the arc-length functional \eqref{arc-eqn} and the Euler-Lagrange equation \eqref{el2-eqn} to prove that the shortest (continuously differentiable) curve between two points in the plane is a straight line. (It’s easy enough to show that the functional is stationary on straight lines—but why are straight lines global minimizers of the functional?)

Of course, the physical significance of extremals of fixed-endpoint functionals derives from Hamilton’s Principle, which is the main topic explored in section (???) above.

In view of the importance of such extremals, it is therefore of interest to understand under what conditions two different fixed-endpoint functionals

\begin{equation}\notag J(y) = \int_a^b F(x,y,y’) \ \d x \quad \text{and} \quad K(y) = \int_a^b G(x,y,y’) \ \d x \end{equation}

determine the same extremals. In the situation that they do have the same extremals, let us agree to say $J$ and $K$ are equivalent. One sufficient condition for equivalence is if $F$ and $G$ differ by a total derivative

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{\d \Phi}{\d x}(x,y) = \frac{\bd \Phi}{\bd x}(x,y) + \frac{\bd \Phi}{\bd y}(x,y) y’, \end{equation}

where $\Phi$ is a smooth function of two variables. (Here, we are again comitting the usual abuse of notation by thinking of $y$ as both an independent variable and a function of $x$.) If it is the case that

\begin{equation}\notag G(x,y,y’) = F(x,y,y’) + \frac{\d \Phi}{\d x}(x,y), \end{equation}

then as you may easily check, the Euler-Lagrange equations for $K$ reduce to those of $J$, and hence the two functionals are equivalent. These simple observations are at the heart of Hamilton-Jacobi theory and canonical transformations, that we consider above in section (???).

Blah!

Suppose $F: M \to N$ is a diffeomorphism. For each $x\in M$, we have the pullback map

\begin{equation}\notag (F^{-1})^\ast_x: T^\ast_xM \to T^\ast_yN,\quad \alpha \mapsto \alpha \circ (F^{-1})_{\ast,y} \end{equation}

where $y = F(x)$.

Theorem. In the notation above, the maps $(F^{-1})^\ast_x$ fit together to yield a smooth vector bundle morphism

\begin{equation}\notag \widehat{F} : T^\ast M \to T^\ast N, \quad (x,\alpha) \mapsto \left(F(x), (F^{-1})^\ast_x(\alpha)\right), \end{equation}

over $F$. In particular, for any point $(x,\alpha) \in T^\ast M$, it is possible to choose local coordinates near $(x,\alpha)$, and local coordinates near $F^\ast(x,\alpha)$, in which $\widehat{F}$ is the identity map.

To obtain local coordinates with the stated properties, simply choose local coordinates near $x$ and local coordinates near $F(x)$ in which $F$ is the identity map. This is possible since $F$ is a diffeomorphism. Use these coordinates to induce local coordinates on the cotangent bundles, in the usual way. Q.E.D.

Corollary. If $F:M \to N$ is a diffeomorphism, then the induced map

\begin{equation}\notag \widehat{F}:T^\ast M \to T^\ast N \end{equation}

on cotangent bundles is a symplectomorphism.

A mathematical interlude: the formula for a general variation

In our section on variational methods, we gave a description of the variation $\delta J_q$ of a functional

\begin{equation}\notag J(q) = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} F(t,q,\dot{q}) \ \d t. \end{equation}

(Here, for notational consistency with what’s coming up in this section, I’ve replaced the interval $[a,b]$ with $[t_1,t_2]$.) The variation $\delta J_q$ is the infinitesimal part of the change in $J$ when we give an infinitesimal nudge to the path $q$ by passing from $q$ to $q+\dev h$:

\begin{equation}\label{diff2-eqn} J(q+\dev h) - J(q) = \delta J_q(h) \dev. \end{equation}

If we conceive of $\delta J_q(h)$ as a type of directional derivative and $h$ a direction vector, then in the formula \eqref{deriv-eqn} that we obtained for $\delta J_q(h)$, we assumed that $h$ pointed in the “vertical” direction, in the sense that it is defined on the same interval $[t_1,t_2]$ as $q$. Then, of course, both integrals in \eqref{diff2-eqn} were computed over this interval:

\begin{equation}\notag J(q) = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} F(t,q,\dot{q}) \ \d t \quad \text{and} \quad J(q+\dev h) = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} F(t,q+\dev h,\dot{q}+\dev \dot{h}) \ \d t. \end{equation}

The goal in this section is to answer the following:

Question. What does a formula for the variation $\delta J_q(h)$ look like if we drop the assumption that $h$ points in the “vertical” direction, and allow a (possible) horizontal displacement?

Here’s the picture that I have in mind:

On the left, we visualize $h$ has a “vertical” direction vector, so that $q+\dev h$ is defined on the same interval as $q$. On the right, we suppose that $h$ is not defined on the same interval as $q$, so that $q+\dev h$ is visualized as an infinitesimal nudge with a nonzero horizontal component.

But wait!

The sum $q+\dev h$ doesn’t make any sense, if $q$ and $h$ are defined on different intervals! Indeed, if I attempt to evaluate the sum

\begin{equation}\label{sense-eqn} q(t) + \dev h(t) \end{equation}

I will get an undefined expression if $t$ lies outside the domain of either $q$ or $h$ Therefore, it appears that the expression \eqref{sense-eqn} only makes sense for those $t$ which lie in the intersection of the domains of $q$ and $h$.

The way out of this pickle is to extend the curves $q$ and $h$ linearly at their endpoints using their tangent lines. Suppose, for example, that we have this situation:

Then we may use the tangent lines of $q$ and $h$ to extend the curves so that they are defined on the same interval:

Pretty slick idea, right?

Now, let’s suppose that $q$ is defined on $[t_1,t_2]$, and that $h$ is defined on a shifted interval $[t_1+\delta t_1,t_2 + \delta t_2]$:

Remember, however, that our ultimate goal is to compute a formula for the variation $\delta J_q(h)$, which is the first-order infinitesimal part of the difference

\begin{equation}\notag J(q + \dev h) - J(q). \end{equation}

The path $\dev h$ is supposed to represent an infinitesimal nudge to the path $q$, which we can make precise by letting $\dev$ be a formal first-order infinitesimal. But $\dev$ only “infinitesimalizes” the vertical part of the nudge; in order to “infinitesimalize” the horizontal part of the nudge, we therefore need to take $\delta t_1$ and $\delta t_2$ as formal first-order infintiesimals as well! So, we are going to have three formal infinitesimal quantities in our computations, namely $\dev$, $\delta t_1$ and $\delta t_2$. Each of these squares to zero, and their pairwise products also vanish:

\begin{equation}\label{pair-eqn} \dev \delta t_1 = \dev \delta t_2 = \delta t_1 \delta t_2 = 0. \end{equation}

In the last figure above, I have labelled the vertical displacements $\delta q_1$ and $\delta q_2$. These too are infinitesimals, expressible in terms of $\dev$ and the $\delta t_i$s. Indeed, notice that

\begin{equation}\notag (q + \dev h)(t_i + \delta t_i) = q(t_i) + \dot{q}(t_i) \delta t_i + \dev (h(t_i) + \dot{h}(t_i)\delta t_i) = q(t_i) + \dot{q}(t_i) \delta t_i + h(t_i)\dev \end{equation}

for $i=1,2$. (Notice that the second equation follows from the fact $\dev \delta t_i = 0$.) Thus, we have computed

\begin{equation}\label{refer-eqn} \delta q_i = \dot{q}(t_i)\delta t_i + h(t_i) \dev, \end{equation}

for each of $i=1,2$.

With these last relations in hand, we now have everything that we need to compute the first-order infinitesimal parts of the difference

\begin{equation}\label{ono-eqn} J(q+\dev h) - J(q). \end{equation}

Let’s begin with the first term which, by definition, is

\begin{equation}\notag J(q + \dev h) = \int_{t_1 + \delta t_1}^{t_2 + \delta t_2} F(t,q+\dev h , \dot{q} + \dev \dot{h}) \ \d t. \end{equation}

But as we saw earlier (equation \eqref{comp-eqn}, to be precise), the integral on the right may be expanded as

\begin{equation}\label{blanket-eqn} \int_{t_1 + \delta t_1}^{t_2 + \delta t_2} F(t,q+\dev h , \dot{q} + \dev \dot{h}) \ \d t = \int_{t_1 + \delta t_1}^{t_2 + \delta t_2} F(t,q, \dot{q}) \ \d t + \dev\int_{t_1 + \delta t_1}^{t_2 + \delta t_2} \left( \frac{\bd F}{\bd q}h + \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}}\dot{h} \right) \ \d t. \end{equation}

Both integrals on the right-hand side of this latter equation contain infinitesimals in their lower and upper limits. However, we have

\begin{equation}\notag \int_{t_1 + \delta t_1}^{t_2 + \delta t_2} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2 + \delta t_2} - \int_{t_1}^{t_1 + \delta t_1} \end{equation}

and so the first integral on the right-hand side of \eqref{blanket-eqn} is

\begin{equation}\label{floor1-eqn} \int_{t_1 + \delta t_1}^{t_2 + \delta t_2} F(t,q, \dot{q}) \ \d t = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} F(t,q,\dot{q}) \ \d t + F|_{t=t_2}\delta t_2 - F|_{t=t_1} \delta t_1, \end{equation}

where by $F|_{t=t_2}\delta t_2$ I mean

\begin{equation}\notag F(t_2,q(t_2),\dot{q}(t_2))\delta t_2 \end{equation}

and similarly for $F|_{t=t_1} \delta t_1$. A similar expansion of the second integral on the right-hand side of \eqref{blanket-eqn} yields

\begin{equation}\label{floor2-eqn} \dev\int_{t_1 + \delta t_1}^{t_2 + \delta t_2} \left( \frac{\bd F}{\bd q}h + \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}}\dot{h} \right) \ \d t = \dev\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left( \frac{\bd F}{\bd q}h + \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}}\dot{h} \right) \ \d t. \end{equation}

In comparing \eqref{floor1-eqn} to \eqref{floor2-eqn}, you might have expected that the right-hand side of \eqref{floor2-eqn} would have two terms containing the infinitesimals $\delta t_1$ and $\delta t_2$, but observe that the left-hand side of \eqref{floor2-eqn} has a factor of $\dev$, and remember the equations \eqref{pair-eqn}.

An application of integration by parts to the right-hand side of \eqref{floor2-eqn} (specifically to the term involving $\dot{h}$), gives us

\begin{equation}\label{floor3-eqn} \dev \int_{t_1}^{t_2 } \left( \frac{\bd F}{\bd q}h + \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}}\dot{h} \right) \ \d t = \dev \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}}h \Big|_{t=t_1}^{t=t_2} + \dev \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\bd F}{\bd q} - \frac{\d }{\d t} \left( \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}}\right) \right] h \ \d t. \end{equation}

Collecting all of our computations in \eqref{blanket-eqn}, \eqref{floor1-eqn} and \eqref{floor3-eqn}, we get that

\begin{equation}\label{almost-eqn} J(q+\dev h) - J(q) = \left( F\delta t + \dev \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}}h\right) \Big|_{t=t_1}^{t=t_2} + \dev \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\bd F}{\bd q} - \frac{\d }{\d t} \left( \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}}\right) \right] h \ \d t, \end{equation}

where we define $\delta t|_{t=t_i} = \delta t_i$.

We are almost finished, equation \eqref{almost-eqn} is very close to giving us our final expression for the infinitesimal part of the difference

\begin{equation}\notag J(q+\dev h) - J(q), \end{equation}

but we need to exchange the terms involving $h$ in the first set of parentheses on the right-hand side of \eqref{almost-eqn} for terms involving the infinitesimals $\delta t_i$ and $\delta q_i$. To do this, we use the relations \eqref{refer-eqn} to replace each $\dev h(t_i)$ in the first set of parentheses on the right-hand side of \eqref{almost-eqn}. The result is:

\begin{align}\label{general-eqn} J(q+\dev h) - J(q) &= \left[ \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}}\delta q - \left(\frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}}\dot{q} - F \right)\delta t \right] \Bigg|_{t=t_1}^{t=t_2} \\ &\hspace{0.5in}+ \dev \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\bd F}{\bd q} - \frac{\d }{\d t} \left( \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}}\right) \right] h \ \d t, \notag \end{align}

where, as with $\delta t$ above, we define $\delta q|_{t=t_i} = \delta q_i$. Now this is precisely the expression that we seek!

Notice that the right-hand side of \eqref{general-eqn} contains multiple purely infinitesimal quantities, namely $\dev$, $\delta t_i$ and $\delta q_i$ (for $i=1,2$). This makes it difficult to identify precisely the piece that should be called the variation $\delta J_q(h)$. In the fixed-endpoint situation, where

\begin{equation}\notag \delta t_i = \delta q_i = 0, \quad i=1,2, \end{equation}

there was no problem in identifying $\delta J_q(h)$, but in the general situation it’s clearly different.

So, even though we began this section seeking a formula for $\delta J_q(h)$ under a general variation, we will not give one, and instead settle for \eqref{general-eqn}. It will turn out that this formula will do just fine, for our purposes!

Exercise. Our derivation of \eqref{general-eqn} assumed that there was only one $q$. If, instead, there are multiple components $q^1,\ldots,q^n$, so that

\begin{equation}\notag J(q^1,\ldots,q^n) = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} F(t,q^1,\ldots,q^n, \dot{q}^1,\ldots,\dot{q}^n) \ \d t, \end{equation}

prove that the computations in this section may be generalized to yield

\begin{align}\notag J(q^1+\dev h^1,\ldots,q^n+\dev h^n) - J(q^1,\ldots,q^n) &= \left[ \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}^i}\delta q^i - \left(\frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}^i}\dot{q}^i - F \right)\delta t \right] \Bigg|_{t=t_1}^{t=t_2} \\ &+ \dev \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\bd F}{\bd q^i} - \frac{\d }{\d t} \left( \frac{\bd F}{\bd \dot{q}^i}\right) \right] h^i \ \d t, \notag \end{align}

with summation over repreated indices implied.

(If you don’t feel like doing this exercise (I wouldn’t want to do it), you could just take my word for it, that these are the equations that you’d obtain. :) Or, you could look up the derivations in Gelfand and Fomin’s book.)

The dual picture: lagrangian systems

We pause our discussion of the classical variational viewpoint on hamiltonian mechanics to introduce the “dual” formalism, known as lagrangian mechanics. Our description here will be rather terse, setting up only the most basic definitions and results that we need in order to continue our discussion from the previous section.

Now, remember that an abstract hamiltonian system was defined to be a triple $(P,\omega,H)$, where $(P,\omega)$ is a symplectic manifold and $H$ is a smooth function in $C^\infty(P)$ called the hamiltonian function or energy function. Such abstract systems arise concretely in mechanics where $P = T^\ast Q$, the phase space over a configuration space $Q$ of some dynamical system, and therefore the hamiltonian $H$ is a smooth function on $T^\ast Q$.

Suppose that we consider instead the tangent bundle $TQ$, which we shall call the velocity phase space in order to distinguish it from the (momentum) phase space $T^\ast Q$. Under certain conditions which I shall describe below, the time evolution of the system can equally well be described by a smooth function $L\in C^\infty(TQ)$, called the lagrangian function. It will turn out that $L$ and $H$ are related to each other via something called a Legendre transform.

Given a general lagrangian $L\in C^\infty(TQ)$, we will obtain the Legendre transform of $L$ via an intermediate gadget called the fiber derivative of $L$. Here’s how it works. First, fix a point $(q,v)$ in $TQ$ consisting of a point $q\in Q$ and a tangent vector $v\in T_q Q$. Recall (or learn, for the first time) that the tangent space $T_qQ$ is a subspace of $T_{(q,v)}(TQ)$; indeed, it is the subspace consisting of all so-called “vertical” tangent vectors, but in order to get everything right, we need to take care in understanding exactly how $T_qQ$ is embedded in $T_{(q,v)}(TQ)$.

The natural projection $\pi: TQ \to Q$ is a submersion. If we identify $T_q Q$ with the fiber $\pi^{-1}(q)$ in the usual way as a (properly embedded) submanifold, then the sequence

\begin{equation}\label{tree-eqn} 0 \to T_{(q,v)}(T_q Q) \to T_{(q,v)}(TQ) \xrightarrow{ \pi_{\ast,(q,v)}} T_q Q \to 0 \end{equation}

is exact. If we choose local coordinates $q^1,\ldots,q^n$ for $q$, then the tangent space $T_qQ$ has the associated coordinate basis

\begin{equation}\label{bush1-eqn} \bd_{q^1},\ldots,\bd_{q^n}, \end{equation}

and so $v$ may be expanded in components as $v = v^i \bd_{q^i}$. The smooth structure on $TQ$ is defined in such a way that

\begin{equation}\label{bush4-eqn} q^1,\ldots,q^n,v^1,\ldots,v^n \end{equation}

serve as local coordinates of $(q,v)$, and so the tangent space $T_{(q,v)}(TQ)$ has the associated coordinate basis

\begin{equation}\label{bush2-eqn} \bd_{q^1},\ldots,\bd_{q^n} , \bd_{v^1},\ldots,\bd_{v^n}. \end{equation}

It is important to understand that $\bd_{q^i}$ means different things between \eqref{bush1-eqn} and \eqref{bush2-eqn}. In \eqref{bush1-eqn}, $q^i$ is understood as a local coordinate function on $Q$, whereas in \eqref{bush2-eqn}, it is understood as a local coordinate function on $TQ$. Now, using exactness of \eqref{tree-eqn}, it is easy to see that

\begin{equation}\notag T_{(q,v)}(T_qQ) = \ker{\pi_{\ast,(q,v)}} \end{equation}

has basis

\begin{equation}\label{bush3-eqn} \bd_{v^1},\ldots,\bd_{v^n}. \end{equation}

These are exactly the vertical tangent (basis) vectors in $T_{(q,v)}(TQ)$, so named because they project to zero under the (pushforward of the) natural map $\pi$.

Now for the crucial point: The vector space $T_qQ$ is identified with a subspace of $T_{(q,v)}(TQ)$ by mapping it isomorphically onto the subspace

\begin{equation}\notag T_{(q,v)}(T_qQ). \end{equation}

In other words, the embedding of $T_qQ$ into $T_{(q,v)}(TQ)$ is obtained by mapping the basis vectors \eqref{bush1-eqn} of the former onto the vectors \eqref{bush3-eqn} in the latter:

\begin{equation}\label{iso-eqn} T_q(Q) \to T_{(q,v)}(TQ), \quad \bd_{q^i} \mapsto \bd_{v^i}. \end{equation}

Now, having taken extreme care in getting the setup just right, defining the fiber derivative is a breeze:

Definition. Let $Q$ be an $n$-dimensional manifold and $L\in C^\infty(TQ)$ a smooth function. The fiber derivative of $L$ is the map

\begin{equation}\notag FL: TQ \to T^\ast Q \end{equation}

defined as follows: Given $(q,v)\in T Q$, we consider the differential

\begin{equation}\notag \d L_{(q,v)}: T_{(q,v)}(TQ) \to \bbr. \end{equation}

Composing this with the embedding \eqref{iso-eqn} yields a linear map that we denote

\begin{equation}\label{fiber-eqn} \d L_{(q,v)}|_{q} : T_qQ \to \bbr, \end{equation}

and the fiber derivative is given as

\begin{equation}\notag (q,v) \mapsto FL(q,v) = (q,\d L_{(q,v)}|_{q} ). \end{equation}

Notice that $\d L_{(q,v)}|_{q}$ is nothing but the restriction of the differential $\d L_{(q,v)}$ to (the tangent space of) the fiber, which explains the name “fiber derivative.”

Theorem (Fiber derivatives in local coordinates). Let $Q$ be an $n$-dimensional manifold and $L\in C^\infty(TQ)$ a smooth function. In the local coordinates

\begin{equation}\notag (q,v) = (q^1,\ldots,q^n,v^1,\ldots,v^n) \end{equation}

of \eqref{bush4-eqn}, the fiber derivative $FL:TQ \to T^\ast Q$ is given by

\begin{equation}\notag (q,v) \mapsto \left( q, \frac{\bd L}{\bd v^1}(q,v),\ldots,\frac{\bd L}{\bd v^n}(q,v) \right). \end{equation}

In particular, $FL$ is a smooth map of manifolds.

Indeed, as you may easily verify, the $1$-form \eqref{fiber-eqn} is expressed in local coordinates as

\begin{equation}\notag \d L_{(q,v)}|_{q} = \frac{\bd L}{\bd v^i}(q,v) \ \d q^i, \end{equation}

with summation over $i$ implied.

Theorem/Definition (Characterizations of regular lagrangians). Let $Q$ be an $n$-dimensional manifold, let $L\in C^\infty(TQ)$ be a smooth function, let

\begin{equation}\notag FL:TQ \to T^\ast Q \end{equation}

be its fiber derivative, and let $\omega$ be the canonical symplectic form on $T^\ast Q$. The following are equivalent:

  1. The function $FL$ is a local diffeomorphism.
  2. The pullback $2$-form $(FL)^\ast \omega$ is a symplectic form on $TQ$.
  3. The function $L$ satisfies the Legendre condition, which means that for each $(q,v)\in TQ$, the $n\times n$ square matrix \begin{equation}\notag \begin{bmatrix} \displaystyle\frac{\bd ^2 L}{\bd v^i \bd v^j}(q,v) \end{bmatrix} \end{equation} is nonsingular.

If $L$ has any one of these properties (and hence all), it is called a regular lagrangian. If $L$ has the stronger property of being a (global) diffeomorphism, it is called a hyperregular lagrangian.

Hamilton-Jacobi theory and canonical transformations

Our treatment of Hamilton-Jacobi theory begins immediately where we just left off, with the expression \eqref{general-eqn} for the general variation of a functional and its multi-variable extension in the previous exercise. In fact, if you look closely at the first term in brackets on the right-hand side of \eqref{general-eqn}, you might recognize something… :)

If you don’t recognize anything, hold on for just a second, and let me set up some notation to bring it out a little clearer.

Let $L(t,q,\dot{q})$ be the lagrangian of some system, and let

\begin{equation}\notag A_L(q) = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} L(t,q,\dot{q}) \ \d t \end{equation}

be the lagrangian action integral. Here, I am assuming that there is only one $q$ for notational brevity, but as usual, everything I am about to describe holds equally well for multiple components $q^1,\ldots,q^n$.

Let’s suppose that we fix a point $(t_0,q_0)$, and assume that for each $(t,q)$ (sufficiently close to $(t_0,q_0)$) there is a unique stationary point of $A_L$, defined on $[t_0,t]$, and going from $q_0$ to $q$. In this way, we obtain a function

\begin{equation}\notag (t,q) \mapsto S(t,q) \end{equation}

called Hamilton’s principal function. For example, suppose that we have:

Here, I’ve drawn the fixed point $(t_0,q_0)$, and three more points along with the (assumed unique!) stationary points of $A_L$ that connect them. Then $S(t,q)$ is the value of the action integral $A_L$ along the displayed path, and similarly for $S(t_1,q_1)$ and $S(t_2,q_2)$.

Assuming that $S(t,q)$ smoothly depends on $t$ and $q$, we might hope to gain some insight into the time evolution of the system by determining exactly how $S$ depends on $t$ and $q$. For example, we might seek to measure the difference

\begin{equation}\label{leaf-eqn} S(t_1,q_1) - S(t,q), \end{equation}

where $(t,q)$ and $(t_1,q_1)$ are displayed above. If we choose $(t_1,q_1)$ close enough to $(t,q)$, then the corresponding pair of paths should differ from each other by an “infinitesimal nudge”, and hence the difference \eqref{leaf-eqn} should be equal to the variation of $A_L$ between the two paths. Thus, from \eqref{general-eqn}, we obtain

\begin{equation}\label{difffan-eqn} \d S = \frac{\bd L}{\bd \dot{q}} \ \d q - \left( \frac{\bd L}{\bd \dot{q}}\dot{q} - L \right) \ \d t, \end{equation}

where we’ve used that stationary points of $A_L$ are solutions to the Euler-Lagrange equation, and so all that remains is the first part in brackets to which I called your attention above.

Now, provided that $L$ is a regular lagrangian, we may perform a Legendre transform to obtain the corresponding hamiltonian, defined so that

\begin{equation}\notag p = \frac{\bd L}{\bd \dot{q}} \quad \text{and} \quad H(q,p) = p\dot{q} - L. \end{equation}

Substituting into \eqref{difffan-eqn}, we get:

\begin{equation}\notag \d S = p \ \d q - H \ \d t. \end{equation}

But this is precisely the action $1$-form $\alpha_H$! It follows then that

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{\bd S}{\bd q} = p \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\bd S}{\bd t} = -H, \end{equation}

and so

\begin{equation}\label{HJE-eqn} H \left( q, \frac{\bd S}{\bd q}\right) + \frac{\bd S}{\bd t} = 0. \end{equation}

This nonlinear partial differential equation is called the ($1$-dimensional) Hamilton-Jacobi equation, to which we have just demonstrated that Hamilton’s principal function is a solution.

Blah 2!

From the dynamics-inspired viewpoint, this means that the law of motion $X$ of a classical dynamical system $(P,X)$ is uniquely determined by its maximal local flow $\theta$. Thus:

Theorem (Classical dynamical systems in terms of flows). Fixing a manifold $P$, there is a one-to-one correspondence between pairs $(P,X)$ and pairs $(P,\theta)$, where $X$ is a vector field on $P$ and $\theta$ is a maximal local flow. The correspondence is given by

\begin{equation}\notag (P,\theta) \mapsto (P,X), \end{equation}

where $X$ is the infinitesimal generator of $\theta$.

The equivalent formulation given in this last theorem of a classical dynamical system in terms of maximal local flows shows that such systems satisfy Arnold’s criteria (see the references in the section on Further Reading) and ultimately justifies our definition:

Arnold’s Criteria for a Classical Dynamical System. A classical dynamical system is any physical system which is smooth, finite-dimensional, and deterministic.

Such a system may be modeled mathematically as a classical dynamical system as defined above, i.e., a pair $(P,X)$ consisting of a smooth manifold $P$ and a vector field $X\in \calx(P)$.

Indeed, the smooth and finite-dimensional properties required by Arnold are modeled in the smoothness and finite-dimensionality of the smooth phase space manifold $P$, while the deterministic property is modeled in the determinism of the maximal local flow $\theta$ induced by $X$, which we described at the beginning of this section.

Of course, pure determinism is a mathematical idealization. In the real world of laboratories and experiments, no instruments of infinite precision exist, which injects a degree of indeterminism into the theory. We will address this in a later post when we talk about observables, states, and measurements in classical mechanics. And beyond classical systems, indeterminism is a built-in feature of mathematical models for quantum dynamical systems, which we will also discuss.

We finish this section with another important class of vector fields which are known to be complete, and will be important below when we discuss symplectic reductions.

Theorem (Completeness and Lie groups). Every left-invariant vector field on a Lie group $G$ is complete.

Let $G$ be a Lie group and $\mfg$ its Lie algebra, identified as the tangent space $T_eG$ at the neutral element $e$. We know that $\mfg$ may also be identified with the vector space $\calx_l(G)$ of all left-invariant vector fields on $G$ via the correspondence

\begin{equation}\notag \mfg \ni \xi \leftrightarrow X \in \calx_l(G), \end{equation}

where the vector field $X$ is obtained from $\xi$ by left-translation, i.e.,

\begin{equation}\notag X_g = (\lambda_g)_{\ast,e}(\xi),\quad g\in G, \end{equation}

where $\lambda_g$ is the left-translation map given by $h\mapsto gh$. Since $X$ is complete, there is a unique global flow

\begin{equation}\notag \theta^\xi : \bbr \times G \to G, \quad (t,g) \mapsto \theta^\xi(t,g) = \theta^\xi_t(g) \end{equation}

for which each smooth curve

\begin{equation}\notag t\mapsto \theta^\xi_t(g) \end{equation}

is an integral curve of $X$ defined on all of $\bbr$. We thus obtain smooth curves

\begin{equation}\notag t \mapsto \exp(t\xi) \defeq \theta^\xi_t(e), \end{equation}

one for each $\xi \in \mfg$, which assemble together to yield the exponential map

\begin{equation}\notag \exp: \mfg \to G, \quad \xi \mapsto \exp(\xi) = \theta^\xi_1(e). \end{equation}

Examples

First off, any finite-dimensional vector space of the form $k^n$ ($=\bbr^n$ or $\bbc^n$) is a Banach space when equipped with its usual norm (prove this!). In fact, any finite-dimensional normed $k$-vector space, whether it be equal to $k^n$ or not, is a Banach space. This is the content of the following very important result:

Theorem. Every finite-dimensional normed $k$-vector space $V$ is a Banach space.

Since $V$ is finite-dimensional, there is a linear isomorphism $V \cong k^n$ for some $n\geq 1$. However, this isomorphism might not be an isometry, in the sense that it might not preserve the norms on $V$ and $k^n$ (the latter equipped with its standard norm). However, it is a general fact that any two norms on a finite-dimensional vector space are equivalent,

Quantum

Quantum systems. A quantum system is defined to be a pair $(A,S)$, where $A$ is a noncommutative, unital $C^\ast$-algebra, and $S$ is the set of all positive, normalized linear functionals on $A$.

  1. The self-adjoint elements in $A$ are called the observables of the system. The algebra $A$ is called the algebra of observables.

  2. The functionals in $S$ are called states of the system. The set $S$ is called the state space of the system.
